defects.anchor_quote: The mean perceived-control score across the 87 survey respondents who answered the item was 3.847
defects.description: GRIM-inconsistent mean: a single-item 1-5 integer rating over N=87 has granularity 1/87; 87 x 3.847 = 334.689 is not an integer sum (nearest achievable means are 334/87 = 3.839 and 335/87 = 3.851), so 3.847 is arithmetically impossible.

defects.anchor_quote: greater perceived control than students in the low-engagement group, t(156) = 3.02, p = .003
defects.description: Degrees of freedom are impossible for the design: an independent-samples t-test on a sample whose maximum N is 142 cannot have df=156; for two groups the correct df is at most N-2=140, and df cannot exceed the sample size.

defects.anchor_quote: the difference was small, t(140) = 1.31, p = .008
defects.description: Reported p-value is inconsistent with the test statistic: t(140)=1.31 gives a two-tailed p of approximately .19 (one-tailed approximately .10), not p=.008; the reported p is far too small and would flip the non-significant result to significant.

defects.anchor_quote: dashboard engagement improved course retention: students who engaged more with the learning analytics dashboard were retained at higher rates
defects.description: Causal claim ('improved', 'raises the probability') drawn from an explicitly cross-sectional, correlational design that cannot support causation; the observed association is confounded and directionally ambiguous.

defects.anchor_quote: Dashboards have been shown to reliably improve outcomes for lower-achieving students
defects.description: The cited source (Ferro & Nakamura, 2021) is titled 'When dashboards demotivate: Peer comparison and the lower-achieving student', which argues the opposite of the claim it is cited to support; the citation is used to back a conclusion contrary to the reference's own stated finding.

defects.anchor_quote: Participants were drawn from the course enrollment using a random sample of students enrolled in the course section
defects.description: Sampling is described as 'random' but the recruitment paragraph two paragraphs later reveals a self-selected volunteer convenience sample (students who chose to respond to an LMS announcement), with no acknowledgment of the contradiction.

defects.anchor_quote: Students were not informed that their dashboard activity data would be analyzed for this study
defects.description: LMS activity logs are analyzed without disclosure or consent (the manuscript states students were not informed), and no IRB/ethics approval or data-governance statement appears anywhere; the survey's own consent covers the survey only, not the log linkage.

defects.anchor_quote: All 142 students in the primary analytic sample were classified into engagement groups for this comparison
defects.description: Table 2 subgroup sizes (High engagement n=66 + Low engagement n=61 = 127) do not sum to the N=142 stated in the prose immediately above the table; 15 students are unaccounted for with no explanation.

defects.anchor_quote: Dashboard engagement correlated positively with retention (r = .42)
defects.description: The Abstract reports the engagement-retention correlation as r=.42 while the Results (Section 4.2) report the same correlation as r=.24; the two values are inconsistent.

defects.anchor_quote: For higher education institutions worldwide, the implication is clear
defects.description: The Conclusion's 'higher education institutions worldwide' recommendation overrides the Limitations section's own single-course, single-interface caveats - the defect is the contradiction between the stated limitations and the worldwide generalization, not an absent hedge.
